  3. “Try random values until the exact optimum is found.”
  4. “Return 0 as the maximum of an empty list.”
  5. “Check only the first three elements” for an arbitrary-length sequence.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Repair termination
docs.text:
Analyse:

def reduce_to_zero(value: int) -> None:
    while value != 0:
        value -= 3

Find inputs for which it terminates and inputs for which it does not. Propose one repaired contract and one repaired algorithm.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Hidden assumptions
docs.text:
The following function finds a maximum:

def maximum(values: list[int]) -> int:
    result = 0
    for value in values:
        if value > result:
            result = value
    return result

State the domain on which it is correct. Then redesign it for all non-empty integer lists.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Correct but inefficient
docs.text: Give two correct algorithms for detecting duplicates in a list. Explain why both are correct and compare their likely time and memory costs.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Partial versus total correctness
docs.text: Describe a procedure that would return the correct answer if it terminated but may fail to terminate. Explain why this establishes at most partial correctness.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Effective primitives
docs.text:
For each operation, explain whether you would treat it as primitive in an introductory algorithm and what cost may be hidden:

  • integer comparison;
  • list indexing;
  • sorting a list;
  • searching a database;
  • asking whether an arbitrary program terminates.

docs.title: 17. Summary
docs.text:
A valid algorithm is more than a sequence of plausible instructions. It has a defined input and required output. Its steps are definite and effective. It terminates on every valid input. It solves the full problem class described by its domain. Its result satisfies the specification. Its resource use can be analysed.

These properties provide a disciplined way to evaluate algorithms before becoming attached to a particular implementation.

The next chapter develops pseudocode as a representation designed to express these properties clearly while remaining independent of Python and C++ syntax.

docs.title: Why pseudocode exists
docs.text:
When we explain an algorithm directly in Python, readers may focus on syntax, library functions, type annotations, or language-specific behaviour. When we explain it only in ordinary prose, the description may be too vague to execute or analyse.

Pseudocode occupies the space between those extremes.

It is a precise but language-independent representation of an algorithm. Its purpose is not to compile. Its purpose is to expose the logic, state, control flow, and data operations clearly enough that the algorithm can be understood, traced, justified, and translated into a real programming language.

Good pseudocode therefore behaves like a carefully designed notation. It is not random English with indentation, and it is not Python with punctuation removed.

What you should learn from this chapter

By the end of the chapter, you should be able to read and write consistent pseudocode using assignment, expressions, conditions, loops, procedures, functions, returns, and data access; translate between pseudocode and Python; and identify pseudocode that is ambiguous, language-dependent, or logically incomplete.

docs.title: 1. The main objective: reveal the algorithm
docs.text:
Consider the problem:

Count how many values in a finite sequence are negative.

A prose description might say:

Go through the values and increase a counter whenever a negative value appears.

A Python implementation might be:

def count_negative(values: list[int]) -> int:
    count = 0
    for value in values:
        if value < 0:
            count += 1
    return count

Pseudocode keeps the algorithmic structure while removing Python-specific syntax:

ALGORITHM count_negative(values)
    count ← 0

    FOR EACH value IN values DO
        IF value < 0 THEN
            count ← count + 1
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN count
END ALGORITHM

The essential ideas remain visible:

  • state variable count;
  • initial value 0;
  • traversal of every input element;
  • condition value < 0;
  • update of the count;
  • returned result.

docs.title: 2. Pseudocode needs explicit conventions
docs.text:
Pseudocode is not governed by one universal syntax. Different books and courses use different styles. That is acceptable only if the conventions are consistent.

This course uses the following rules.

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text:
x ← expression

The left arrow means: evaluate the expression and store the result in x.

docs.title: Equality comparison
docs.text:
x = y

The equality symbol is used in conditions, not for assignment.

docs.title: Inequality
docs.text:
x ≠ y
x < y
x ≤ y
x > y
x ≥ y

docs.title: Boolean operators
docs.text:
AND
OR
NOT

docs.title: Blocks
docs.text:
Blocks are indented and explicitly closed:

IF condition THEN
    ...
END IF

docs.title: Indexing
docs.text: Unless stated otherwise, sequences use zero-based indexing.

docs.title: Return
docs.text:
RETURN value

RETURN ends the current function or algorithm immediately and produces the specified result.

docs.title: Comments
docs.text:
// comment

Comments explain intent but do not form part of the executable method.

docs.title: 3. Variables and assignment
docs.text:
Pseudocode variables represent components of algorithm state.

count ← 0
current_maximum ← values[0]
index ← index + 1

Assignment must not be confused with mathematical equality.

index ← index + 1

means:

  1. read the old value of index;
  2. add one;
  3. replace the old value by the result.

docs.title: Simultaneous assignment
docs.text:
Sometimes two values must be updated without destroying information.

A swap may be written:

temporary ← left
left ← right
right ← temporary

Some pseudocode styles allow:

(left, right) ← (right, left)

This course prefers the explicit temporary-variable version when teaching state changes, because it exposes the sequence of operations.

docs.title: 4. Expressions
docs.text: Expressions produce values.

docs.title: Arithmetic expressions
docs.text:
a + b
length * width
sum / count
value MOD 2

MOD denotes the remainder operation.

docs.title: Boolean expressions
docs.text:
value < 0
index < length(values)
left ≤ right
values[index] = target

docs.title: Combined conditions
docs.text:
index ≥ 0 AND index < length(values)
NOT found
value < minimum OR value > maximum

Parentheses should be used when precedence could be unclear.

(valid_index AND matches) OR allow_missing

A precise condition is preferable to relying on assumed operator precedence.

docs.title: 5. Sequence
docs.text:
A sequence executes instructions in order.

width ← 5
height ← 4
area ← width * height
RETURN area

Later instructions see the state created by earlier instructions.

Changing order may change the result.

x ← 2
y ← x + 1
x ← 10

produces y = 3, while:

x ← 2
x ← 10
y ← x + 1

produces y = 11.

Pseudocode should therefore preserve meaningful ordering explicitly.

docs.title: 6. Selection
docs.text: Selection chooses one path based on a Boolean condition.

docs.title: One-way selection
docs.text:
IF value < 0 THEN
    negative_count ← negative_count + 1
END IF

If the condition is false, the body is skipped.

docs.title: Two-way selection
docs.text:
IF first ≥ second THEN
    maximum ← first
ELSE
    maximum ← second
END IF

Exactly one branch executes.

docs.title: Multi-way selection
docs.text:
IF score ≥ 90 THEN
    grade ← "A"
ELSE IF score ≥ 80 THEN
    grade ← "B"
ELSE IF score ≥ 70 THEN
    grade ← "C"
ELSE
    grade ← "D"
END IF

The conditions are checked in order. The ordering matters because earlier branches may make later checks unnecessary.

docs.title: Avoid overlapping ambiguity
docs.text:
Suppose the specification distinguishes negative, zero, and positive values.

Clear version:

IF value < 0 THEN
    category ← "negative"
ELSE IF value = 0 THEN
    category ← "zero"
ELSE
    category ← "positive"
END IF

The cases are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.

docs.title: 7. Iteration over a sequence
docs.text:
For-each iteration expresses traversal when the element values matter more than their indices.

FOR EACH value IN values DO
    total ← total + value
END FOR

This means that every element is processed once in sequence order.

docs.title: When an index is needed
docs.text:
Use an indexed loop:

FOR index ← 0 TO length(values) - 1 DO
    IF values[index] = target THEN
        RETURN index
    END IF
END FOR

The convention here is that both range endpoints are included.

This is why the final index is written as:

length(values) - 1

docs.title: Empty ranges
docs.text:
If length(values) = 0, then the range from 0 to -1 contains no indices. The loop body executes zero times.

A course or document must state such conventions explicitly because different pseudocode styles interpret ranges differently.

docs.title: 8. While loops
docs.text:
A WHILE loop repeats while a condition is true.

WHILE index < length(values) DO
    process values[index]
    index ← index + 1
END WHILE

A correct while loop usually needs three elements:

  1. initialisation;
  2. condition;
  3. progress update.

Example:

index ← 0

WHILE index < length(values) DO
    IF values[index] = target THEN
        RETURN index
    END IF

    index ← index + 1
END WHILE

RETURN -1

docs.title: A common error: missing progress
docs.text:
index ← 0

WHILE index < length(values) DO
    IF values[index] = target THEN
        RETURN index
    END IF
END WHILE

If the first element does not match, index never changes. The loop repeats forever.

Pseudocode must make progress visible.

docs.title: 9. Repeat-until loops
docs.text:
Some pseudocode traditions include:

REPEAT
    ...
UNTIL condition

The body executes at least once, and repetition stops when the condition becomes true.

Example:

REPEAT
    value ← read_value()
UNTIL value ≥ 0

This differs from:

WHILE value < 0 DO
    value ← read_value()
END WHILE

because the REPEAT body is guaranteed to execute once.

This course uses WHILE and FOR as the primary loop forms unless a post-test loop clarifies the algorithm.

docs.title: 10. Functions and procedures
docs.text:
A named algorithm may return a value.

FUNCTION absolute_value(number)
    IF number < 0 THEN
        RETURN -number
    ELSE
        RETURN number
    END IF
END FUNCTION

A procedure may perform an action without returning a meaningful value.

PROCEDURE swap_first_two(values)
    temporary ← values[0]
    values[0] ← values[1]
    values[1] ← temporary
END PROCEDURE

The second procedure modifies its input. Its contract must make the side effect explicit.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Parameters represent input supplied to the named computation.

FUNCTION first_index(values, target)

A pseudocode convention should explain whether parameters are passed by value, by reference, or abstractly. In this course, parameters are treated abstractly unless mutation is stated explicitly.

docs.title: 11. Returning versus printing
docs.text:
These are not equivalent.

RETURN result

passes a value back to the caller.

PRINT result

produces an output side effect.

A function that prints a number may still return no useful value. Pseudocode should preserve this distinction.

Incorrectly replacing RETURN with PRINT changes the interface.

docs.title: Sequence indexing
docs.text: values[index]

docs.title: Sequence length
docs.text: length(values)

docs.title: Record fields
docs.text:
student.name
student.score

docs.title: Updating a component
docs.text:
values[index] ← new_value
student.score ← student.score + 1

These operations are abstract. Later modules examine their actual memory representation and cost.

docs.title: 13. Sentinel values and failure
docs.text:
A search may return a distinguished value when no result exists.

RETURN -1

This is valid only if -1 cannot be confused with a successful index.

Alternative pseudocode may use:

RETURN NOT_FOUND

or:

RAISE NotFoundError

The failure convention belongs to the contract and should be stated before the pseudocode.

docs.title: 14. Full example: first index of the minimum
Contract
docs.text:
Input:
    values — a finite non-empty sequence of comparable values

Output:
    the smallest index of a minimum value

Side effects:
    none

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
ALGORITHM first_index_of_minimum(values)
    best_index ← 0

    FOR index ← 1 TO length(values) - 1 DO
        IF values[index] < values[best_index] THEN
            best_index ← index
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN best_index
END ALGORITHM

docs.title: Why the comparison is strict
docs.text:
If an equal minimum appears later, the earlier index must be preserved. Therefore the condition is:

<

not:

≤

docs.title: Python translation
docs.text:
def first_index_of_minimum(values: list[int]) -> int:
    if not values:
        raise ValueError("values must not be empty")

    best_index = 0

    for index in range(1, len(values)):
        if values[index] < values[best_index]:
            best_index = index

    return best_index

The pseudocode and Python preserve the same algorithmic state and update rule.

docs.title: 15. Full example: counting a range
docs.text:
Problem:

Count how many elements satisfy lower ≤ value ≤ upper.

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
ALGORITHM count_in_range(values, lower, upper)
    count ← 0

    FOR EACH value IN values DO
        IF lower ≤ value AND value ≤ upper THEN
            count ← count + 1
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN count
END ALGORITHM

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def count_in_range(values: list[int], lower: int, upper: int) -> int:
    count = 0

    for value in values:
        if lower <= value <= upper:
            count += 1

    return count

Python allows chained comparison syntax. The pseudocode writes the logical conjunction explicitly.

This is a useful reminder: translation need not be character-for-character. It must preserve meaning.

docs.title: 16. Full example: in-place reversal
docs.text:
Problem:

Reverse a finite sequence in place.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Precondition:
    values is a finite mutable sequence

Postcondition:
    for every valid index i,
    values[i] equals old(values[length(values) - 1 - i])

Side effects:
    values is modified

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
ALGORITHM reverse_in_place(values)
    left ← 0
    right ← length(values) - 1

    WHILE left < right DO
        temporary ← values[left]
        values[left] ← values[right]
        values[right] ← temporary

        left ← left + 1
        right ← right - 1
    END WHILE
END ALGORITHM

The pseudocode exposes:

  • two boundary indices;
  • one swap per iteration;
  • inward progress;
  • termination when the indices meet or cross.

The detailed correctness argument belongs to later chapters, but the representation already supports it.

docs.title: Mistake 1: mixing assignment and equality
docs.text:
Ambiguous:

x = x + 1

Preferred:

x ← x + 1

docs.title: Mistake 2: using undefined operations
docs.text:
make the list efficient

The action has no precise meaning.

docs.title: Mistake 3: hiding a complex algorithm in one verb
docs.text:
sort values optimally

This avoids describing the actual method.

docs.title: Mistake 4: language-dependent syntax
docs.text:
for i in range(len(values)):

This is Python syntax.

docs.title: Mistake 5: unclear range boundaries
docs.text:
FOR i FROM 0 TO n

Does this execute n or n + 1 times? The convention must be stated.

docs.title: Mistake 6: omitting the failure path
docs.text: A search that only shows what happens when the target is found is incomplete.

docs.title: Mistake 7: writing comments instead of operations
docs.text:
// somehow find the correct index

A comment cannot replace the algorithm.

docs.title: 18. Translating pseudocode into Python
docs.text: Use the following method.

docs.title: Step 1: preserve the contract
docs.text: Do not change the input domain, result, failure behaviour, or side effects accidentally.

docs.title: Step 2: map state variables
docs.text: Every important pseudocode variable should have a clear Python counterpart.

docs.title: Step 3: map control structures
docs.text:
• IF → if;
  • ELSE → else;
  • FOR EACH → Python for;
  • indexed FOR → range;
  • WHILE → while;
  • RETURN → return.

docs.title: Step 4: check boundary conventions
docs.text:
An inclusive pseudocode range may require a different Python endpoint because range excludes its stop argument.

Pseudocode:

FOR index ← 1 TO n - 1 DO

Python:

for index in range(1, n):

docs.title: Step 5: account for language semantics
docs.text: Ask whether operations copy, mutate, allocate, or raise exceptions in Python.

docs.title: Step 6: test the translation against traces
docs.text: The same input should produce the same state progression and result.

docs.title: 19. What you must be able to explain
docs.text:
After this chapter, you should be able to:

  • explain the purpose of pseudocode;
  • use assignment and equality consistently;
  • write sequence, selection, and iteration;
  • distinguish FOR EACH, indexed FOR, and WHILE;
  • write functions, procedures, and returns;
  • distinguish returned results from side effects;
  • state indexing and range conventions;
  • translate pseudocode into Python without changing the contract;
  • identify ambiguous or language-specific pseudocode.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Translate to pseudocode
docs.text: Write pseudocode for a Python function that returns the sum of positive values.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Translate to Python
docs.text:
Translate:

ALGORITHM contains_zero(values)
    FOR EACH value IN values DO
        IF value = 0 THEN
            RETURN TRUE
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN FALSE
END ALGORITHM

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Repair the loop
docs.text:
Find and fix the error:

index ← 0
WHILE index < length(values) DO
    PRINT values[index]
END WHILE

docs.title: Exercise 4 — First versus last occurrence
docs.text:
Write pseudocode for both:

  • first index of a target;
  • last index of a target.

Explain how control flow or update rules differ.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — In-place update
docs.text: Write a contract and pseudocode for adding one to every element of a mutable sequence.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Range conventions
docs.text:
Explain how the pseudocode loop:

FOR i ← 0 TO n - 1 DO

translates to Python and C++.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Ambiguity audit
docs.text:
Rewrite this as precise pseudocode:

Look through the values until something interesting appears; otherwise stop at the end.

First define what “interesting” means and what result is required.

docs.title: 21. Summary
docs.text:
Pseudocode is a disciplined representation of an algorithm. It removes unnecessary programming-language syntax while preserving the state, conditions, updates, control flow, and results needed for understanding and reasoning.

Good pseudocode is:

  • consistent;
  • precise;
  • explicit about assignment;
  • clear about ranges and indexing;
  • independent of one programming language;
  • complete on success and failure paths;
  • suitable for tracing and translation.

The next chapter shows how sequence, selection, iteration, and decomposition can be combined systematically to structure larger algorithms.

docs.title: Module 01 Review — Foundations of Algorithm Design
Purpose of this review
docs.text:
This review is not a vocabulary quiz alone. It checks whether you can connect specification, state, representation, control flow, correctness, termination, and complexity.

You should be able to explain your answers without relying only on familiar Python syntax.

docs.title: 1. Core concepts
docs.text:
Explain each term in your own words and give one example:

  1. computational problem;
  2. problem instance;
  3. algorithm;
  4. program;
  5. execution;
  6. program state;
  7. expression;
  8. statement;
  9. assignment;
  10. control flow;
  11. precondition;
  12. postcondition;
  13. side effect;
  14. failure behaviour;
  15. definiteness;
  16. effectiveness;
  17. finiteness;
  18. correctness;
  19. generality;
  20. efficiency.

For every pair below, explain the difference:

  • problem and instance;
  • algorithm and program;
  • program and execution;
  • expression and statement;
  • assignment and equality;
  • returned result and side effect;
  • precondition and validation;
  • test and proof;
  • partial correctness and total correctness;
  • algorithm and representation.

docs.title: 2. Reading state transitions
docs.text:
Initial state:

x = 3
y = 8
z = 0

Instructions:

z ← x + y
x ← z - x
y ← z - y

Answer:

  1. What is the state after each instruction?
  2. What are the final values?
  3. What relationship does the procedure establish between the final values and the initial values?
  4. Which old values are lost?

docs.title: 3. Assignment is not equality
docs.text:
Explain why:

count ← count + 1

is valid pseudocode but:

count = count + 1

is not a valid mathematical equation for ordinary numbers.

Then describe the three operational stages of the assignment.

docs.title: 4. Contracts
docs.text: Write contracts for the following problems.

docs.title: A. Absolute value
docs.text: Return the absolute value of an integer.

docs.title: B. First element
docs.text:
Return the first element of a sequence.

Decide how empty input is handled.

docs.title: C. Index of a target
docs.text: Return the first matching index or a failure result.

docs.title: D. Average
docs.text: Return the arithmetic mean of a sequence of real numbers.

docs.title: E. In-place increment
docs.text:
Add one to every element of a mutable integer sequence.

For each contract include:

  • input;
  • precondition;
  • postcondition;
  • side effects;
  • failure behaviour.

docs.title: 5. Weak and strong conditions
docs.text:
For a function that returns the minimum of a non-empty integer list, evaluate each proposed precondition.

  1. values is a list;
  2. values is a non-empty list of integers;
  3. values contains at least two positive integers;
  4. values is sorted;
  5. length(values) > 0.

Classify each as:

  • insufficient;
  • appropriate;
  • unnecessarily strong;
  • relevant only with additional type assumptions.

Then evaluate these postconditions:

  1. result is an integer;
  2. result <= every element of values;
  3. result is an element of values and result <= every element of values;
  4. result = values[0].

docs.title: 6. Representation comparison
docs.text:
Represent the problem “count values equal to zero” in four forms:

  1. structured natural language;
  2. pseudocode;
  3. Python;
  4. trace table for [0, 4, 0, -2].

Then explain what each form makes easy to see and what it hides.

docs.title: 7. Pseudocode translation
docs.text:
Translate into Python:

ALGORITHM last_index(values, target)
    result ← -1

    FOR index ← 0 TO length(values) - 1 DO
        IF values[index] = target THEN
            result ← index
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN result
END ALGORITHM

Explain why the algorithm returns the last occurrence rather than the first.

docs.title: A. Missing progress
docs.text:
def contains(values: list[int], target: int) -> bool:
    index = 0

    while index < len(values):
        if values[index] == target:
            return True

    return False

Identify the defect, give a failing input, and repair the function.

docs.title: B. Invalid initial state
docs.text:
def minimum(values: list[int]) -> int:
    result = 0

    for value in values:
        if value < result:
            result = value

    return result

State the domain on which it works and redesign it for every non-empty integer list.

docs.title: C. Wrong tie rule
docs.text:
def first_index_of_maximum(values: list[int]) -> int:
    best_index = 0

    for index in range(1, len(values)):
        if values[index] >= values[best_index]:
            best_index = index

    return best_index

Give a counterexample and explain which problem the function actually solves.

docs.title: D. Side effect mismatch
docs.text:
def doubled(values: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    for index in range(len(values)):
        values[index] *= 2
    return values

Explain why the name and return type do not reveal the full contract. Write two clearer alternatives:

  • one producing a new list;
  • one modifying the input in place.

docs.title: 9. Correctness reasoning
docs.text:
For:

def count_even(values: list[int]) -> int:
    count = 0

    for value in values:
        if value % 2 == 0:
            count += 1

    return count

Write:

  1. the state meaning of count;
  2. the initialisation argument;
  3. the preservation argument for even and odd values;
  4. the completion argument;
  5. the termination argument;
  6. time and auxiliary-space complexity.

docs.title: 10. Structured design problem
docs.text:
Design a function:

Given a non-empty list of integers, return the difference between its maximum and minimum.

Complete every stage:

  1. contract;
  2. boundary cases;
  3. state meanings;
  4. structured prose;
  5. pseudocode;
  6. Python;
  7. trace for [7, 2, 9, 4];
  8. correctness argument;
  9. termination argument;
  10. complexity.

Do not use Python's built-in min or max.

docs.title: 11. Decomposition problem
docs.text:
Design a small score-processing program with these requirements:

  • input is a non-empty list of scores;
  • every score must be between 0 and 100;
  • return the average;
  • return the minimum and maximum;
  • return how many scores are passing under threshold 50;
  • do not modify the input.

Propose function boundaries. For each function state:

  • responsibility;
  • precondition;
  • postcondition;
  • side effects;
  • complexity.

Then explain how the contracts compose in the main function.

docs.title: 12. Property diagnosis
docs.text:
For each procedure, identify the principal violated or missing property.

  1. “Continue until the output seems accurate.”
  2. “Check the first item and return it as the maximum.”
  3. “Keep adding one while x != 0.”
  4. “Sort the list instantly.”
  5. A program works for ten tests but no general argument is available.
  6. A procedure returns the correct result but may run forever.
  7. The output is described only as “some useful value.”

Possible concepts include:

  • definiteness;
  • effectiveness;
  • finiteness;
  • generality;
  • correctness;
  • specification completeness.

docs.title: 13. Complexity basics
docs.text: Analyse time and auxiliary space.

docs.title: A
docs.text:
def first(values: list[int]) -> int:
    return values[0]

docs.title: B
docs.text:
def total(values: list[int]) -> int:
    result = 0
    for value in values:
        result += value
    return result

docs.title: C
docs.text:
def copy_values(values: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    result = []
    for value in values:
        result.append(value)
    return result

docs.title: D
docs.text:
def contains_pair(values: list[int], target: int) -> bool:
    for first in values:
        for second in values:
            if first + second == target:
                return True
    return False

For each analysis state:

  • input-size measure;
  • important operation;
  • best and worst case when they differ;
  • auxiliary memory.

docs.title: 14. Capstone problem
docs.text:
Design an algorithm for the following specification:

Given a finite sequence of integers, return the first index at which the running sum becomes strictly negative. Return -1 if the running sum never becomes negative.

Example:

[4, -1, -2, -5, 8] -> 3

because the running sums are:

4, 3, 1, -4, 4

Your solution must include:

  1. precise contract;
  2. explanation of the term “running sum”;
  3. state variables and meanings;
  4. pseudocode;
  5. Python;
  6. trace;
  7. correctness argument;
  8. termination argument;
  9. complexity;
  10. tests for empty input, immediate failure, no failure, and later failure.

docs.title: 15. Oral self-check
docs.text:
Without looking back, answer aloud:

  1. Why must an algorithm be separated from its program?
  2. What is state?
  3. Why is initialisation a logical claim?
  4. How do preconditions and postconditions divide responsibility?
  5. Why is testing not proof?
  6. What makes pseudocode useful?
  7. Why does a trace describe an execution rather than the whole algorithm?
  8. How do sequence, selection, iteration, and decomposition support reasoning?
  9. What is the difference between correctness and efficiency?
  10. How can one character such as > versus >= change the problem solved?

docs.title: 16. Mastery criteria
docs.text:
Module 01 is mastered when you can independently:

  • formulate a precise problem and contract;
  • identify state and state transitions;
  • distinguish expressions, statements, and assignments;
  • choose representations deliberately;
  • write consistent pseudocode;
  • design with sequence, selection, iteration, and decomposition;
  • trace execution;
  • explain correctness and termination;
  • analyse basic time and auxiliary-space complexity;
  • identify boundary cases, invalid inputs, side effects, and failure behaviour;
  • translate an algorithm into Python without changing its specification.

If any of these still depends on memorising one example, return to the relevant chapter before continuing.

docs.title: 17. Transition to Module 02
docs.text:
Module 01 established the language of algorithm design. Module 02 develops the first major control mechanisms in depth:

  • iterative loops;
  • loop termination conditions;
  • recursion;
  • construction of correct algorithms;
  • primitive and compound data types.

The concepts from Module 01 remain active. Every loop and recursive function will still require a contract, meaningful state, a termination argument, a correctness argument, and a complexity analysis.

docs.title: Why structure matters
docs.text:
A correct idea can become difficult to understand when it is expressed as a long, tangled sequence of instructions. The problem is not only aesthetic. Poor structure makes it harder to see what the algorithm is doing, harder to test individual parts, harder to reason about correctness, and easier to introduce hidden interactions between variables.

Structured algorithm design addresses this problem by building solutions from a small number of well-understood forms:

  • sequence — perform steps in order;
  • selection — choose among alternatives;
  • iteration — repeat a block;
  • decomposition — divide a larger task into named subproblems.

These forms are sufficient to express a very large class of algorithms. Their value comes from predictability. Each has a clear entry, a clear exit, and a clear role in the reasoning.

What you should learn from this chapter

By the end of the chapter, you should be able to design a small algorithm using sequence, selection, iteration, and decomposition; explain how state moves through each structure; recognise unnecessary nesting and duplicated logic; and refactor a monolithic procedure into smaller parts with explicit responsibilities.

docs.title: 1. Start from the problem, not from the control structure
docs.text:
Control structures are tools. They should be chosen because the problem requires a certain form of reasoning.

Consider the task:

Given a finite sequence of integers, return the sum of the positive values and the number of negative values.

The requirements suggest:

  • every value must be examined;
  • positive and negative values require different actions;
  • two independent summaries must be maintained;
  • zero contributes to neither result.

From those requirements, the structure follows naturally:

  1. initialise two state variables;
  2. iterate through the sequence;
  3. select one of the relevant cases;
  4. update the corresponding state;
  5. return both results.

We do not begin by deciding “I want a loop.” We identify repeated work, then choose iteration.

docs.title: 2. Sequence: state changes in order
docs.text:
A sequence executes instructions one after another.

positive_sum ← 0
negative_count ← 0

The order of these two assignments does not matter because they are independent.

Now consider:

average ← total / count
RETURN average

Here the order matters. average must be computed before it can be returned.

docs.title: Data dependencies
docs.text:
A later instruction may depend on state produced earlier.

width ← 5
height ← 4
area ← width * height

The final assignment depends on both earlier assignments.

A useful question is:

Which values must already exist before this instruction can execute correctly?

This reveals the data dependency structure of the sequence.

docs.title: Accidental order dependencies
docs.text:
Poorly designed code sometimes creates unnecessary dependencies through reused variables.

result = width * height
result = result + border
result = result * price_per_unit

The same variable represents three different meanings over time. The code may be correct, but reasoning is harder.

Clearer version:

area = width * height
area_with_border = area + border
cost = area_with_border * price_per_unit

Structure includes naming state so that each stage has a visible meaning.

docs.title: 3. Selection: express distinct logical cases
docs.text:
A selection chooses which instructions execute based on a condition.

For the motivating task:

IF value > 0 THEN
    positive_sum ← positive_sum + value
ELSE IF value < 0 THEN
    negative_count ← negative_count + 1
END IF

Zero causes no update.

docs.title: Conditions should come from the specification
docs.text:
The cases are not arbitrary. They correspond to three mathematical categories:

value > 0
value < 0
value = 0

The first two require actions. The third requires none.

docs.title: Exhaustive and mutually exclusive cases
docs.text:
Good case analysis asks:

  • Do the cases cover every valid possibility?
  • Can more than one case apply at the same time?

For sign classification:

value < 0
value = 0
value > 0

The cases are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.

docs.title: Independent conditions versus exclusive branches
docs.text:
Compare:

if value > 0:
    positive_count += 1

if value % 2 == 0:
    even_count += 1

A value may be both positive and even, so two independent if statements are appropriate.

By contrast:

if value < 0:
    category = "negative"
elif value == 0:
    category = "zero"
else:
    category = "positive"

Only one category applies, so an exclusive chain is appropriate.

Using elif where conditions are not exclusive can silently skip required actions.

docs.title: 4. Iteration: repeat one meaningful step
docs.text:
Iteration is appropriate when the same logical operation must be applied repeatedly.

FOR EACH value IN values DO
    ...
END FOR

The loop should have a clear interpretation.

For our task:

After processing a prefix of the sequence:
    positive_sum is the sum of positive values in that prefix
    negative_count is the number of negative values in that prefix

Each iteration extends the processed prefix by one element while preserving those meanings.

docs.title: The loop body should represent one unit of progress
docs.text:
A good loop body answers:

What changes when one additional element is processed?

For the current value:

  • if positive, add it to positive_sum;
  • if negative, increment negative_count;
  • if zero, change nothing.

docs.title: Full pseudocode
docs.text:
ALGORITHM summarise_signs(values)
    positive_sum ← 0
    negative_count ← 0

    FOR EACH value IN values DO
        IF value > 0 THEN
            positive_sum ← positive_sum + value
        ELSE IF value < 0 THEN
            negative_count ← negative_count + 1
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN (positive_sum, negative_count)
END ALGORITHM

docs.title: Python implementation
docs.text:
def summarise_signs(values: list[int]) -> tuple[int, int]:
    positive_sum = 0
    negative_count = 0

    for value in values:
        if value > 0:
            positive_sum += value
        elif value < 0:
            negative_count += 1

    return positive_sum, negative_count

The implementation mirrors the conceptual structure directly.

docs.title: 5. While loops and explicit progress
docs.text:
A for loop is useful when the iteration set is known. A while loop is useful when repetition continues until a condition changes.

Example:

Repeatedly divide a positive integer by two using integer division until it becomes zero, and count the steps.

def halving_steps(number: int) -> int:
    if number <= 0:
        raise ValueError("number must be positive")

    steps = 0

    while number > 0:
        number //= 2
        steps += 1

    return steps

The loop structure contains:

  • initial state: positive number, steps = 0;
  • continuation condition: number > 0;
  • progress: integer division reduces number;
  • bound: number cannot remain a positive integer forever while being halved;
  • final meaning: steps counts the performed reductions.

docs.title: A while loop must expose progress
docs.text:
Bad structure:

while number > 0:
    steps += 1

The condition depends on number, but number never changes. The loop does not progress.

When reading a while loop, identify which statement moves the state toward termination.

docs.title: 6. Nesting structures
docs.text:
Algorithms often combine sequence, selection, and iteration.

Example:

For every row of a matrix, count how many values are negative.

def negative_counts(rows: list[list[int]]) -> list[int]:
    result: list[int] = []

    for row in rows:
        count = 0

        for value in row:
            if value < 0:
                count += 1

        result.append(count)

    return result

Structure:

outer iteration over rows
    initialise one row count
    inner iteration over values
        selection for negative values
    store row result

docs.title: Nesting should reflect problem hierarchy
docs.text: The outer loop corresponds to rows. The inner loop corresponds to values within one row. The nesting is meaningful because the data itself is nested.

docs.title: Unnecessary nesting
docs.text:
Poor version:

if values:
    for value in values:
        if value > 0:
            if value % 2 == 0:
                result.append(value)

The logic may be flattened:

for value in values:
    if value > 0 and value % 2 == 0:
        result.append(value)

The clearer form depends on whether the separate conditions have distinct conceptual roles. Nesting should reveal logic, not merely accumulate indentation.

docs.title: 7. Decomposition: divide the problem into named responsibilities
docs.text:
A large algorithm becomes easier to understand when it is divided into smaller functions or procedures.

Suppose we need to process student scores:

  1. validate that every score is between 0 and 100;
  2. compute the average;
  3. determine whether the average is passing;
  4. return a summary.

Monolithic version:

def build_summary(scores: list[float]) -> dict[str, object]:
    if not scores:
        raise ValueError("scores must not be empty")

    for score in scores:
        if score < 0 or score > 100:
            raise ValueError("score out of range")

    total = 0.0
    for score in scores:
        total += score

    average = total / len(scores)

    if average >= 50:
        passed = True
    else:
        passed = False

    return {"average": average, "passed": passed}

This is understandable, but several responsibilities are mixed.

Decomposed version:

def validate_scores(scores: list[float]) -> None:
    if not scores:
        raise ValueError("scores must not be empty")

    for score in scores:
        if score < 0 or score > 100:
            raise ValueError("score out of range")


def average(scores: list[float]) -> float:
    total = 0.0
    for score in scores:
        total += score
    return total / len(scores)


def has_passed(average_score: float) -> bool:
    return average_score >= 50


def build_summary(scores: list[float]) -> dict[str, object]:
    validate_scores(scores)
    average_score = average(scores)

    return {
        "average": average_score,
        "passed": has_passed(average_score),
    }

docs.title: Benefits of decomposition
docs.text:
Each function now has:

  • one primary responsibility;
  • a smaller contract;
  • fewer state variables;
  • simpler tests;
  • a clearer correctness argument;
  • potential reuse.

docs.title: Decomposition is not fragmentation
docs.text:
Too many tiny functions can also harm readability.

A function should represent a meaningful operation or decision. Creating a separate function for every arithmetic expression adds indirection without clarifying the design.

Useful question:

Does this part have a coherent responsibility that can be named and specified independently?

docs.title: 8. Contracts connect decomposed parts
docs.text:
When one function calls another, the caller relies on the callee's contract.

average_score = average(scores)

The caller needs to know:

  • whether scores may be empty;
  • whether the function modifies scores;
  • what value is returned;
  • what errors may occur.

Decomposition works well only when interfaces are explicit.

A large proof can then be organised compositionally:

  1. validation establishes the precondition for average;
  2. average guarantees a valid mean;
  3. has_passed classifies the mean;
  4. build_summary combines the guaranteed results.

This is one reason modularity supports correctness.

docs.title: 9. Avoid duplicated logic
docs.text:
Duplication creates multiple places where the same rule must remain consistent.

Bad example:

if score >= 50:
    print("pass")

# later
if score > 50:
    save_result("pass")

The two conditions differ. One treats 50 as passing, the other does not.

Better:

def has_passed(score: float) -> bool:
    return score >= 50

Then both uses depend on one definition.

docs.title: Duplication can hide semantic drift
docs.text:
Two blocks may begin identical and later evolve differently. Centralising a meaningful rule reduces that risk.

Do not remove duplication mechanically. Extract shared logic when the duplicated code represents the same concept.

docs.title: 10. Guard clauses and early return
docs.text:
A guard clause handles a special or invalid case early.

Nested version:

def first_value(values: list[int]) -> int:
    if values:
        return values[0]
    else:
        raise ValueError("values must not be empty")

Guard-clause version:

def first_value(values: list[int]) -> int:
    if not values:
        raise ValueError("values must not be empty")

    return values[0]

The second version separates failure from the main path and reduces nesting.

docs.title: Early return in search
docs.text:
def contains(values: list[int], target: int) -> bool:
    for value in values:
        if value == target:
            return True

    return False

The algorithm stops as soon as the answer is known.

docs.title: Early return versus single exit
docs.text:
Multiple returns can improve clarity when they correspond to decisive outcomes. They can also make reasoning harder if scattered unpredictably through a large procedure.

Use early return when it clarifies the contract and control flow.

docs.title: 11. State locality
docs.text:
State should exist in the smallest scope that needs it.

Bad design:

count = 0


def count_positive(values: list[int]) -> int:
    global count
    count = 0

    for value in values:
        if value > 0:
            count += 1

    return count

The result depends on global mutable state.

Better:

def count_positive(values: list[int]) -> int:
    count = 0

    for value in values:
        if value > 0:
            count += 1

    return count

Local state:

  • is easier to reason about;
  • avoids interference between calls;
  • makes the function easier to test;
  • reduces side effects.

Module 05 develops side effects and function interaction with the environment in greater depth.

docs.title: 12. Refactoring a poorly structured algorithm
docs.text:
Task:

Return the average of non-negative values, or None if no non-negative value exists.

Poor version:

def average_non_negative(values: list[int]) -> float | None:
    result = 0
    x = 0
    for y in values:
        if y >= 0:
            result = result + y
            x = x + 1
    if x == 0:
        return None
    else:
        return result / x

The algorithm is correct, but naming and structure are weak.

Improved version:

def average_non_negative(values: list[int]) -> float | None:
    total = 0
    count = 0

    for value in values:
        if value >= 0:
            total += value
            count += 1

    if count == 0:
        return None

    return total / count

Improvements:

  • names expose state meanings;
  • initialisation is grouped;
  • the loop body expresses one case;
  • the no-result case is handled by a guard clause;
  • the main return is visually clear.

docs.title: Further decomposition?
docs.text:
A separate function is not necessary unless the filtering or averaging rule needs independent reuse or testing.

Refactoring should improve conceptual structure, not maximise function count.

docs.title: 13. Structure and correctness reasoning
docs.text: Structured code supports structured reasoning.

docs.title: Sequence
docs.text: Show that each step establishes what the next step requires.

docs.title: Selection
docs.text: Show that every possible case is covered and that each branch establishes the required condition.

docs.title: Iteration
docs.text:
Show:

  • initialisation;
  • invariant preservation;
  • progress;
  • termination;
  • postcondition at completion.

docs.title: Decomposition
docs.text:
Use the contracts of smaller functions to justify the larger function.

This correspondence is one of the main reasons structured programming matters in algorithmics.

docs.title: 14. Structure and complexity
docs.text: Structure also guides cost analysis.

docs.title: Sequence
docs.text:
Costs add:

O(n) + O(n) = O(n)

docs.title: Selection
docs.text: For worst-case analysis, consider the more expensive branch.

docs.title: Iteration
docs.text: Multiply the number of iterations by the cost of the body, then account for changing body costs when necessary.

docs.title: Nested iteration
docs.text: Two nested loops over n elements often suggest O(n²), but only if the inner loop executes n times for each outer iteration.

docs.title: Function calls
docs.text:
Include the cost of called functions. A one-line call may hide substantial work.

Structure does not automatically determine complexity, but it provides the skeleton of the analysis.

docs.title: Mixing unrelated responsibilities
docs.text: One function validates input, reads files, transforms data, prints results, and updates global state.

docs.title: Excessive nesting
docs.text: Deeply nested branches make the main path difficult to see.

docs.title: Unclear loop state
docs.text: Variables change, but their intended meanings are not stated.

docs.title: Repeated conditions
docs.text: The same business or mathematical rule appears in multiple places with slight differences.

docs.title: Hidden side effects
docs.text: A helper function modifies input even though its name suggests a calculation.

docs.title: Premature decomposition
docs.text: Every tiny expression becomes a separate function, making the algorithm fragmented.

docs.title: Control structure chosen before the problem is understood
docs.text: The author forces recursion, a loop, or a library operation without first deriving the need from the specification.

docs.title: 16. A practical structuring workflow
docs.text:
For a new algorithm:

  1. write the contract;
  2. identify the main states or phases;
  3. place dependent steps in sequence;
  4. identify genuinely different cases;
  5. identify repeated work and its progress measure;
  6. assign a precise meaning to each persistent state variable;
  7. extract coherent subproblems into functions;
  8. make side effects visible;
  9. remove duplicated rules;
  10. trace the structured algorithm;
  11. justify each structure;
  12. analyse cost by following the structure.

docs.title: 17. What you must be able to explain
docs.text:
After this chapter, you should be able to:

  • derive sequence, selection, and iteration from problem requirements;
  • distinguish exclusive and independent conditions;
  • state the meaning of loop state;
  • identify progress in a while loop;
  • explain when nesting reflects data structure and when it obscures logic;
  • decompose a task into meaningful functions;
  • connect function contracts during composition;
  • use guard clauses and early returns deliberately;
  • keep state local;
  • refactor a monolithic algorithm without changing its behaviour;
  • use structure to support correctness and complexity analysis.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Derive the structure
docs.text:
Design an algorithm that returns:

  • the number of even values;
  • the sum of odd values.

State why iteration and selection are needed.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Exclusive or independent?
docs.text:
For each pair of conditions, decide whether to use two independent if statements or an if/elif chain:

  1. value is positive; value is even;
  2. score is below 50; score is at least 50;
  3. record is invalid; record should be logged;
  4. number is negative; number is zero; number is positive.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Refactor nesting
docs.text:
Rewrite:

def collect(values: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    result = []
    if values:
        for value in values:
            if value >= 0:
                if value % 2 == 0:
                    result.append(value)
    return result

Explain every structural change.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Decompose a task
docs.text: Design a program that validates temperatures, computes minimum and maximum, and formats a report. Propose meaningful function boundaries and contracts.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Find the progress measure
docs.text:
For each loop, identify what changes and why termination follows:

  • scanning an array by increasing index;
  • repeatedly dividing a positive integer by two;
  • moving left upward and right downward;
  • removing one item from a queue each iteration.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Detect duplicated logic
docs.text: Write two examples where duplicated conditions may drift apart. Refactor each into one named rule.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Structure a proof
docs.text:
For average_non_negative, state:

  • the loop invariant;
  • the completion argument;
  • the no-result case;
  • time and auxiliary-space complexity.

docs.title: 19. Summary
docs.text:
Structured algorithms are built from sequence, selection, iteration, and decomposition. These are not merely programming-language constructs. They are reasoning forms.

Sequence expresses dependency. Selection expresses cases. Iteration expresses repeated progress. Decomposition expresses responsibility and composition.

Good structure makes state meanings visible, isolates side effects, reduces duplication, supports testing, clarifies correctness, and guides complexity analysis.

The next chapter combines the complete Module 01 toolkit—contracts, representations, properties, pseudocode, and structure—in fully worked examples.

docs.title: Worked Examples: From Specification to Python
Why worked examples matter
docs.text:
Definitions become useful only when they guide real decisions. This chapter combines the ideas from Module 01 into complete solution workflows.

Each example follows the same structure:

  1. problem statement;
  2. contract;
  3. representation of the central idea;
  4. state design;
  5. pseudocode;
  6. Python implementation;
  7. manual trace;
  8. correctness argument;
  9. termination argument;
  10. complexity analysis;
  11. boundary cases and common mistakes.

The examples are deliberately small. Their purpose is to establish a disciplined method that later applies to searching, sorting, recursion, trees, graphs, and more complex data structures.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given three integers, return the greatest value.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Input:
    first, second, third — integers

Precondition:
    all three inputs are comparable integers

Postcondition:
    result is equal to one of the inputs
    result >= first
    result >= second
    result >= third

Side effects:
    none

The postcondition says more than “return a large value.” It requires the result to be one of the actual inputs and at least as large as every input.

docs.title: Central idea
docs.text: Use the first value as the current candidate, then compare the remaining values with it.

docs.title: State meaning
docs.text: maximum = the greatest value among the inputs processed so far

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
ALGORITHM maximum_of_three(first, second, third)
    maximum ← first

    IF second > maximum THEN
        maximum ← second
    END IF

    IF third > maximum THEN
        maximum ← third
    END IF

    RETURN maximum
END ALGORITHM

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def maximum_of_three(first: int, second: int, third: int) -> int:
    maximum = first

    if second > maximum:
        maximum = second

    if third > maximum:
        maximum = third

    return maximum

docs.title: Trace
docs.text:
Input:

first = 4, second = 9, third = 7
Step maximum before Condition maximum after Initialisation — — 4 Compare second 4 9 > 4 true 9 Compare third 9 7 > 9 false 9

Result: 9.

docs.title: Correctness
docs.text:
After initialisation, maximum is the greatest value among the first processed input, namely first.

After comparing second, maximum is the greatest of first and second.

After comparing third, maximum is the greatest of all three inputs.

Therefore the returned value satisfies the postcondition.

docs.title: Termination
docs.text: The algorithm contains a fixed finite number of comparisons and assignments. It always terminates.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Time: O(1)
Auxiliary space: O(1)

The input size is fixed at three values.

docs.title: Common mistake
docs.text: Using maximum = 0 is incorrect for inputs such as (-4, -9, -2) because zero is not one of the inputs.

docs.title: Example 2 — Count values greater than a threshold
Problem
docs.text: Given a finite sequence of integers and a threshold, return the number of elements strictly greater than the threshold.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Input:
    values — a finite sequence of integers
    threshold — an integer

Precondition:
    none beyond type and finiteness assumptions

Postcondition:
    result equals the number of indices i such that
    0 <= i < length(values) and values[i] > threshold

Side effects:
    values is not modified

docs.title: State meaning
docs.text: count = the number of processed values greater than threshold

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
ALGORITHM count_greater(values, threshold)
    count ← 0

    FOR EACH value IN values DO
        IF value > threshold THEN
            count ← count + 1
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN count
END ALGORITHM

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def count_greater(values: list[int], threshold: int) -> int:
    count = 0

    for value in values:
        if value > threshold:
            count += 1

    return count

docs.title: Trace
docs.text:
Input:

values = [3, 10, 7, 12, 10]
threshold = 9
Value Greater than 9? count before count after 3 No 0 0 10 Yes 0 1 7 No 1 1 12 Yes 1 2 10 Yes 2 3

Result: 3.

docs.title: Correctness
docs.text:
Initially no values have been processed, so count = 0 is correct.

For each new value:

  • if it is greater than the threshold, incrementing adds exactly one newly qualifying element;
  • otherwise leaving count unchanged is correct.

After all values are processed, count equals the required number for the entire sequence.

docs.title: Termination
docs.text: The sequence is finite and each element is processed once.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
For n = len(values):

Time: O(n)
Auxiliary space: O(1)

docs.title: Boundary cases
docs.text:
• empty sequence → result 0;
  • every value greater → result n;
  • no value greater → result 0;
  • values equal to threshold do not count because the comparison is strict.

docs.title: Example 3 — First index of a target
Problem
docs.text: Given a finite sequence and a target, return the first matching index or -1 if no match exists.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Postcondition:
    if target occurs, result is the smallest valid index i
    such that values[i] = target

    otherwise result = -1

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
ALGORITHM first_index(values, target)
    FOR index ← 0 TO length(values) - 1 DO
        IF values[index] = target THEN
            RETURN index
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN -1
END ALGORITHM

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def first_index(values: list[int], target: int) -> int:
    for index in range(len(values)):
        if values[index] == target:
            return index

    return -1

docs.title: Why early return is correct
docs.text: Indices are examined in increasing order. Therefore the first matching index encountered is necessarily the smallest matching index.

docs.title: Trace
docs.text:
values = [8, 3, 5, 3]
target = 3
Index Value Match? Action 0 8 No continue 1 3 Yes return 1

The later match at index 3 is never examined because the required answer is already known.

docs.title: Correctness
docs.text:
If the algorithm returns index i, then values[i] = target. Since all earlier indices were checked and did not match, i is the first occurrence.

If the algorithm returns -1, every valid index was checked and none matched, so the target is absent.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Best case: O(1)
Worst case: O(n)
Auxiliary space: O(1)

docs.title: Example 4 — Minimum and maximum in one pass
Problem
docs.text: Given a non-empty finite sequence of integers, return both its minimum and maximum.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Precondition:
    length(values) > 0

Postcondition:
    minimum is an element of values
    maximum is an element of values
    minimum <= every element
    maximum >= every element

Side effects:
    values is not modified

docs.title: State meanings
docs.text:
minimum = the smallest processed value
maximum = the greatest processed value

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
ALGORITHM minimum_and_maximum(values)
    minimum ← values[0]
    maximum ← values[0]

    FOR index ← 1 TO length(values) - 1 DO
        value ← values[index]

        IF value < minimum THEN
            minimum ← value
        END IF

        IF value > maximum THEN
            maximum ← value
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN (minimum, maximum)
END ALGORITHM

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def minimum_and_maximum(values: list[int]) -> tuple[int, int]:
    if not values:
        raise ValueError("values must not be empty")

    minimum = values[0]
    maximum = values[0]

    for value in values[1:]:
        if value < minimum:
            minimum = value

        if value > maximum:
            maximum = value

    return minimum, maximum

docs.title: Implementation note
docs.text:
values[1:] creates a new list in Python. A version avoiding that additional allocation is:

def minimum_and_maximum(values: list[int]) -> tuple[int, int]:
    if not values:
        raise ValueError("values must not be empty")

    minimum = values[0]
    maximum = values[0]

    for index in range(1, len(values)):
        value = values[index]

        if value < minimum:
            minimum = value

        if value > maximum:
            maximum = value

    return minimum, maximum

docs.title: Correctness
docs.text: Both state meanings are true for the one-element processed prefix. Each new value can only lower the minimum, raise the maximum, or leave them unchanged. After the full sequence is processed, both postconditions hold.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Index-based version:

Time: O(n)
Auxiliary space: O(1)

Slicing version:

Time: O(n)
Auxiliary space: O(n) because of the slice

The high-level algorithm is the same, but implementation details affect memory cost.

docs.title: Example 5 — Reverse a sequence in place
Problem
docs.text: Reverse a mutable sequence without creating another sequence of the same size.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Precondition:
    values is a finite mutable sequence

Postcondition:
    for every valid index i,
    values[i] = old(values[length(values) - 1 - i])

Side effects:
    values is modified

docs.title: State design
docs.text:
Use two indices:

left = first position not yet fixed
right = last position not yet fixed

Swap the two values, then move both indices inward.

docs.title: Pseudocode
docs.text:
ALGORITHM reverse_in_place(values)
    left ← 0
    right ← length(values) - 1

    WHILE left < right DO
        temporary ← values[left]
        values[left] ← values[right]
        values[right] ← temporary

        left ← left + 1
        right ← right - 1
    END WHILE
END ALGORITHM

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def reverse_in_place(values: list[int]) -> None:
    left = 0
    right = len(values) - 1

    while left < right:
        values[left], values[right] = values[right], values[left]
        left += 1
        right -= 1

docs.title: Trace
docs.text:
Input:

[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
Step left right Sequence after swap Initial 0 4 [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] 1 0 4 [5, 2, 3, 4, 1] 2 1 3 [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]

The loop stops when left = 2 and right = 2.

docs.title: Correctness idea
docs.text:
After each iteration:

  • positions before left contain their final reversed values;
  • positions after right contain their final reversed values;
  • the unprocessed region lies between left and right.

When the indices meet or cross, no unfixed pair remains.

docs.title: Termination
docs.text: left increases and right decreases. The distance between them shrinks by two each iteration, so the loop terminates.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Time: O(n)
Auxiliary space: O(1)

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a non-empty list of scores from 0 to 100, return the arithmetic mean. Reject invalid input.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Precondition for successful computation:
    length(scores) > 0
    every score satisfies 0 <= score <= 100

Postcondition:
    result = sum(scores) / length(scores)

Failure behaviour:
    raise ValueError when the input is empty or contains an invalid score

Side effects:
    scores is not modified

docs.title: Decomposition
docs.text:
Separate validation from computation.

def validate_scores(scores: list[float]) -> None:
    if not scores:
        raise ValueError("scores must not be empty")

    for score in scores:
        if score < 0 or score > 100:
            raise ValueError("scores must be between 0 and 100")


def average_score(scores: list[float]) -> float:
    validate_scores(scores)

    total = 0.0
    for score in scores:
        total += score

    return total / len(scores)

docs.title: Why decomposition helps
docs.text:
validate_scores has one responsibility: establish the domain conditions.

average_score can then rely on those conditions when dividing by the length and interpreting values as valid scores.

docs.title: Correctness
docs.text: Validation ensures the list is non-empty and all values are in the permitted range. The accumulation loop computes the sum of all scores. Division by the number of scores therefore produces the arithmetic mean.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Validation scans the list once and summation scans it once:

O(n) + O(n) = O(n)

Auxiliary space remains O(1).

docs.title: Cross-example lessons
docs.text: The examples use different control structures, but the reasoning pattern is stable.

docs.title: Contracts prevent accidental problem changes
docs.text:
• “first occurrence” requires preserving earlier equal values;
  • “non-empty” justifies initialisation from the first element;
  • “in place” requires mutation and limits extra memory;
  • “strictly greater” excludes equality;
  • failure behaviour determines whether a sentinel or exception is used.

docs.title: State meanings guide implementation
docs.text:
• count represents the number of qualifying processed elements;
  • best_index identifies the best processed candidate;
  • minimum and maximum summarise a processed prefix;
  • left and right delimit unfinished work;
  • total represents a partial sum.

docs.title: Correctness follows the structure
docs.text:
• sequence establishes prerequisites for later steps;
  • selection handles logically distinct cases;
  • iteration extends a processed region;
  • decomposition composes smaller contracts.

docs.title: Complexity follows operations and representation
docs.text: A short line may hide allocation or a library algorithm. Analyse the implementation actually used, not only the number of source lines.

docs.title: What you must be able to do
docs.text:
After studying these examples, you should be able to take a similarly small problem and produce:

  1. a precise contract;
  2. a central idea in prose;
  3. state-variable meanings;
  4. pseudocode;
  5. Python implementation;
  6. trace table;
  7. correctness argument;
  8. termination argument;
  9. time and auxiliary-space analysis;
  10. boundary-case analysis.

The final chapter of Module 01 now tests whether those skills can be used independently rather than followed passively.

docs.title: Module 02 — Iteration, Recursion, and Data Types
Why this module matters
docs.text:
The first module established how algorithms are specified, represented, structured, and justified. Module 02 now develops two of the most important mechanisms for expressing repeated computation: iteration and recursion.

Both mechanisms allow a finite description to process input whose size is not known in advance. A loop can process every element of a list regardless of whether the list contains ten values or ten million. A recursive function can reduce a problem to a smaller instance of the same kind until a simple base case is reached.

The module also introduces the distinction between primitive and compound data types. Repetition does not operate in a vacuum: every algorithm manipulates values, and the type and structure of those values determine which operations are meaningful, how data is represented, and what costs are incurred.

docs.title: Syllabus scope
docs.text:
This module develops the second course-content block:

Iterative loops and their termination conditions. Recursion. Constructing a correct algorithm. Primitive and compound data types.

The sequence is deliberate. We begin with loops because they make repeated state changes visible. We then study termination because repetition is useful only when it eventually stops. Recursion is introduced as a different way to express repeated structure. The module closes by connecting both control mechanisms to data types and to a disciplined method of constructing correct algorithms.

docs.title: What you should be able to do after this module
docs.text:
After completing the module, you should be able to:

  1. explain the difference between iteration and recursion;
  2. identify the state changed by a loop;
  3. distinguish definite iteration from condition-controlled iteration;
  4. formulate a loop termination condition;
  5. explain why a loop terminates using a decreasing measure;
  6. identify and repair infinite loops and off-by-one errors;
  7. define a recursive problem using a base case and a recursive case;
  8. trace recursive calls and returns;
  9. explain why a recursive definition terminates;
  10. compare iterative and recursive solutions to the same problem;
  11. distinguish primitive from compound data types;
  12. explain how data type choices affect algorithm design;
  13. construct a simple correct algorithm from specification to implementation;
  14. give introductory correctness, termination, time, and space arguments.

docs.title: Concept map
docs.text:
repeated computation
        │
        ├── iteration
        │      ├── loop state
        │      ├── initialisation
        │      ├── condition
        │      ├── update
        │      ├── invariant
        │      └── termination measure
        │
        └── recursion
               ├── problem reduction
               ├── base case
               ├── recursive case
               ├── call stack
               └── progress toward the base case

values manipulated by both approaches
        │
        ├── primitive types
        │      ├── integers
        │      ├── real numbers
        │      ├── booleans
        │      └── characters
        │
        └── compound types
               ├── sequences
               ├── records
               ├── tuples
               └── nested structures

docs.title: 1. Iteration as repeated state transformation
docs.text: Learn how a loop repeatedly transforms state and how to read the four essential components: initialisation, condition, body, and update.

docs.title: 2. for loops and definite iteration
docs.text: Study loops whose number of iterations is determined by a finite sequence or range. Learn indexing, processed prefixes, and common boundary errors.

docs.title: 3. while loops and condition-controlled iteration
docs.text: Study loops whose number of repetitions depends on the changing state. Learn how to design conditions and updates that express the intended process.

docs.title: 4. Loop termination and correctness
docs.text: Learn how to justify that a loop stops, how to use a decreasing measure, and how invariants connect the current state to the final result.

docs.title: 5. Recursion
docs.text: Learn how a problem can be defined in terms of smaller instances of itself. Study base cases, recursive cases, progress, and the call stack.

docs.title: 6. Iteration versus recursion
docs.text: Compare equivalent solutions, examine trade-offs, and learn when recursive structure clarifies a problem and when iteration is operationally simpler.

docs.title: 7. Primitive and compound data types
docs.text: Study the categories of values manipulated by algorithms, their operations, their constraints, and their influence on algorithm design.

docs.title: 8. Constructing a correct algorithm
docs.text: Combine specification, examples, state design, invariants, termination, implementation, testing, and complexity into a repeatable workflow.

docs.title: 9. Worked examples and review
docs.text: Apply the complete method to counting, searching, digit processing, Euclid's algorithm, exponentiation, and recursive decomposition.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
You should already be able to:

  • identify input, output, state, and side effects;
  • write simple preconditions and postconditions;
  • read assignments and conditional statements;
  • trace a short imperative program;
  • understand sequence and selection;
  • read basic Python functions.

docs.title: Diagnostic questions
docs.text:
Before beginning, try to answer:

  1. What must change during every iteration of a terminating while loop?
  2. Why can a loop condition be true initially and still guarantee termination?
  3. What is the difference between a loop invariant and a termination condition?
  4. Why does a recursive function need a base case?
  5. What does it mean for a recursive call to make progress?
  6. Why can a mathematically valid recursive definition still be a poor implementation?
  7. Is a Python list a primitive or compound value?
  8. How can the same problem be solved both iteratively and recursively?

Return to these questions after completing the module.

docs.title: Mastery checklist
docs.text:
You have mastered this module when you can honestly say:

  • [ ] I can identify all components of a loop.
  • [ ] I can explain what information a loop state represents.
  • [ ] I can trace both for and while loops.
  • [ ] I can detect an off-by-one error.
  • [ ] I can identify a decreasing termination measure.
  • [ ] I can state and use a simple loop invariant.
  • [ ] I can define a recursive solution with a base and recursive case.
  • [ ] I can trace recursive calls and returns.
  • [ ] I can explain why a recursive process terminates.
  • [ ] I can compare iterative and recursive complexity.
  • [ ] I can distinguish primitive and compound data types.
  • [ ] I can construct and justify a small correct algorithm.

Study objective

Do not stop when a loop or recursive function produces the right answer for one example. The objective is to explain the state, progress, correctness, termination, boundary cases, and cost.

docs.title: Why repetition needs a model
docs.text:
Many algorithms perform the same kind of operation more than once. A program may inspect every value in a sequence, repeatedly reduce a number, continue reading until valid input is obtained, or search until a target is found.

It is tempting to describe all of these situations simply by saying: “use a loop.” That phrase names a programming construct, but it does not explain the algorithm. To understand iteration, we need to answer more precise questions:

  • What information exists before the loop starts?
  • What does one iteration accomplish?
  • Which part of the state changes?
  • What remains true after every iteration?
  • What causes the loop to stop?
  • Why does the final state represent the required result?

Iteration is best understood as repeated state transformation.

What you should learn from this chapter

By the end of the chapter, you should be able to identify the initial state, loop condition, body, update, processed portion of the input, and final state of a simple iterative algorithm.

docs.title: 1. A problem that cannot be solved by one fixed expression
docs.text:
Consider the task:

Given a finite list of integers, compute the sum of its elements.

For the concrete input:

[4, 7, -2, 5]

a human may write:

4 + 7 - 2 + 5 = 14

But the number of values is not fixed. Another input may contain one element, one thousand elements, or none. We therefore need a finite description that can adapt to the size of the input.

The key idea is to remember a partial result.

After processing the first element, remember its contribution. After processing the second, update the remembered result. Continue until every element has been incorporated.

This gives the Python function:

def sum_values(values: list[int]) -> int:
    total = 0

    for value in values:
        total = total + value

    return total

The code is short. The algorithmic meaning is richer.

docs.title: 2. The four essential parts of iteration
docs.text: Every simple loop can be analysed through four components.

docs.title: Initialisation
docs.text:
Before repetition begins, the algorithm establishes an initial state.

total = 0

docs.title: Repetition rule
docs.text:
The loop determines which items or states will be processed.

for value in values:

docs.title: Body and update
docs.text:
The body uses the current information to create the next state.

total = total + value

docs.title: Completion
docs.text:
When no iterations remain, the algorithm uses the final state as the result.

return total

These parts must fit together. A correct body cannot repair an initial state that represents the wrong fact. A meaningful initial state is useless if the loop omits part of the input. A loop that maintains the correct result may still be wrong if it never stops.

docs.title: 3. The meaning of the accumulator
docs.text:
The variable total is often called an accumulator because it accumulates contributions from successive input elements.

Its intended meaning is:

total = the sum of all input elements processed so far

This sentence is more important than the variable name. It allows us to explain every phase of the algorithm.

Before the loop, no elements have been processed, and the sum of no selected values is 0. Therefore the initialisation is correct.

During an iteration, value is the next unprocessed element. Adding it to total extends the partial sum to include one more element.

After the loop, every element has been processed. Therefore the partial sum is the sum of the entire input.

This pattern is not specific to summation. The same shape appears in many algorithms:

count = number of matching elements processed so far
product = product of elements processed so far
maximum = greatest element processed so far
found = whether a target has appeared among processed elements

A good iterative algorithm gives each state variable a precise meaning.

docs.title: 4. Iteration creates a sequence of states
docs.text:
For the input:

[4, 7, -2, 5]

the execution can be represented as:

total = 0
    ↓ process 4
total = 4
    ↓ process 7
total = 11
    ↓ process -2
total = 9
    ↓ process 5
total = 14

The loop is therefore not one action repeated in a vague sense. It creates a sequence of well-defined states:

state₀ → state₁ → state₂ → state₃ → state₄

A trace table makes the same process explicit:

Iteration Current value total before total after Processed prefix Initial — — 0 [] 1 4 0 4 [4] 2 7 4 11 [4, 7] 3 -2 11 9 [4, 7, -2] 4 5 9 14 [4, 7, -2, 5]

The phrase processed prefix refers to the part of the sequence already examined from the beginning up to the current position.

docs.title: 5. The boundary between processed and unprocessed data
docs.text:
A useful mental model divides the input into two regions:

processed | unprocessed

At the beginning:

[] | [4, 7, -2, 5]

After two iterations:

[4, 7] | [-2, 5]

At the end:

[4, 7, -2, 5] | []

The loop advances this boundary one element at a time. The state summarises the processed region.

For the sum algorithm:

total = sum(processed region)

For a maximum algorithm:

current_maximum = greatest value in processed region

For a search algorithm:

found = target occurs in processed region

This model becomes essential when studying loop invariants and correctness.

docs.title: 6. Empty input is not always an error
docs.text:
For summation, the empty list has a natural result:

sum_values([]) == 0

This is consistent with the state meaning. Before the loop, no values have been processed and total is 0. The loop executes zero times, so the result remains 0.

This illustrates a useful fact:

A loop may execute zero times and still be correct.

Whether empty input is valid depends on the problem.

For a sum, the empty result can naturally be 0. For a product, the corresponding identity is often 1. For a maximum, an empty input has no greatest element unless the specification provides a separate convention.

The correct initial state therefore depends on both the operation and the input domain.

docs.title: 7. Counting is a related but different pattern
docs.text:
Consider:

def count_even(values: list[int]) -> int:
    count = 0

    for value in values:
        if value % 2 == 0:
            count = count + 1

    return count

The state meaning is:

count = the number of even values processed so far

The loop still advances through the list one element at a time, but not every element changes the state.

If the current value is even, the count increases. If it is odd, the count remains unchanged. In both cases, the meaning of count remains true after the current element becomes part of the processed prefix.

This is an important point: doing nothing in one branch is still part of the algorithm. The unchanged state must also be justified.

docs.title: 8. Search may stop before processing the whole input
docs.text:
Not every loop must examine every element.

def contains(values: list[int], target: int) -> bool:
